summary: Jeff Atwood argues that code itself is a liability, not an asset, and that developers should strive to write as little of it as possible. Building on Rich Skrenta's assertion that 'code is our enemy,' Atwood reframes the problem: the real enemy isn't the code but the developers who write too much of it. He advocates for brevity as the starting point for all programming decisions, arguing that every line of code represents future debugging, maintenance, and cognitive load. The post uses a simple example (String.Empty vs "") to illustrate how developers often prefer verbosity for misguided reasons. Atwood concludes with a paradox: truly loving code means writing as little of it as possible.
key_points: Code is a liability that rots, requires maintenance, hides bugs, and increases organizational complexity
Developers are their own worst enemy because they love writing code too much
Programming involves constant trade-offs between brevity, features, speed, robustness, and time spent
The correct approach is to start with brevity and only increase other dimensions when testing reveals the need
Reducing code volume directly reduces the cognitive load required to understand a program
money_quotes: As a software developer, you are your own worst enemy. The sooner you realize that, the better off you'll be.
The best code is no code at all.
Every new line of code you willingly bring into the world is code that has to be debugged, code that has to be read and understood, code that has to be supported.
Every time you write new code, you should do so reluctantly, under duress, because you completely exhausted all your other options.
If you love writing code – really, truly love to write code – you'll love it enough to write as little of it as possible.
Code is only our enemy because there are so many of us programmers writing so damn much of it.
We never met a problem we couldn't solve with some duct tape, a jury-rigged coat hanger, and a pinch of code.
key_insight: The measure of a great programmer is not how much code they can write, but how little code they need to solve a problem.
